original_hypothesis.title: Guardian Proofs Blocked During Proving Pause Prevents Emergency State Correction
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: TaikoL1.proveBlock() is gated by both whenNotPaused and whenProvingNotPaused modifiers. GuardianProver.approve() calls TaikoL1.proveBlock() when the approval threshold is met. This means guardians—the highest-authority tier intended to be the ultimate arbiter—cannot submit corrective proofs when proving is paused. The protocol has no separate guardian-only proving path, creating a deadlock scenario where pausing prevents the very mechanism needed to resolve the issue that caused the pause.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Guardians are designed as the emergency backstop for the protocol. Blocking their ability to prove during a proving pause defeats this purpose and could extend protocol stalls indefinitely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proveBlock has whenProvingNotPaused modifier; no alternative path for guardian-tier proofs

preconditions: Guardians need to submit a corrective proof for a disputed block
No alternative guardian proving function exists

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: whenProvingNotPaused modifier blocks all proofs including guardian tier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No guardian-tier exception in the proving pause logic
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No separate proveBlock path or modifier exception for TIER_GUARDIAN

analysis_questions: Can the owner unpause and guardians prove atomically?
What is the maximum delay between pausing and being able to submit guardian proofs?
Could this deadlock prevent protocol finality indefinitely?
validation_requirements: Confirm GuardianProver.approve calls TaikoL1.proveBlock which checks whenProvingNotPaused
Verify no alternative guardian proving path exists
Assess impact on block verification chain when proving is paused

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Malicious prover submits invalid proof. 2) chain_pauser pauses proving to prevent further damage. 3) Guardians accumulate approvals but cannot submit the corrective proof because proveBlock reverts with L1_PROVING_PAUSED. 4) Protocol stalls: can't verify new blocks, can't fix invalid proof. 5) Must unpause to fix, but unpausing also allows the malicious prover to act again.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol stall blocks all verification; provers' bonds locked; L2 chain progression halted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner must unpause; but no guarantee malicious actors won't exploit the unpause window

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for guardian proving during proving-paused state

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via reverted guardian approval transactions

validation_result.mechanism: guardian proving blocked by proving pause
validation_result.impact_summary: Guardian provers—the highest-authority emergency backstop—cannot submit corrective proofs when proving is paused, creating a deadlock where the emergency mechanism is disabled precisely when it's most needed.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis is validated through direct code tracing. TaikoL1.proveBlock() at lines 72-83 carries both `whenNotPaused` and `whenProvingNotPaused` modifiers. The `whenProvingNotPaused` modifier at lines 29-32 checks `state.slotB.provingPaused` and reverts with `L1_PROVING_PAUSED()` if true. GuardianProver.approve() calls `ITaikoL1(resolve("taiko", false)).proveBlock(...)` when the guardian approval threshold is met. This external call hits the `whenProvingNotPaused` modifier and reverts. No alternative guardian-only proving path exists anywhere in the codebase. The guardian tier (TIER_GUARDIAN with contestBond=0) is designed as the ultimate arbiter that can override any lower-tier proof, yet it is subject to the same proving pause as all other tiers. This means when proving is paused (presumably due to an emergency like a malicious proof), the very mechanism intended to resolve such emergencies is disabled. The owner must unpause proving first, but unpausing also re-enables potentially malicious provers, creating a race condition.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence confirms all aspects of the hypothesis. The modifier chain on proveBlock, the GuardianProver.approve() external call path, and the absence of any guardian-specific bypass are all verified in source code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The whenProvingNotPaused modifier blocks ALL proof submissions when proving is paused, with no exception for the guardian tier. This prevents the highest-authority emergency mechanism from functioning during emergencies.
The modifier checks provingPaused without any caller/tier exception. No check for guardian tier or guardian prover address exists.

When guardian threshold is reached, this function calls TaikoL1.proveBlock() which will revert if proving is paused. The approval bits are deleted before the call, but the external call reverts, reverting the entire transaction including the deleteApproval. However, guardians still cannot submit their proof.

calling_sequence: Guardian calls GuardianProver.approve() with TIER_GUARDIAN proof
Internal Guardians.approve() records approval bit and checks threshold
When minGuardians threshold is met, approved_ = true
state.slotB.provingPaused is true -> reverts with L1_PROVING_PAUSED()
Entire transaction reverts including the deleteApproval and approval recording
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: GuardianProver.approve() -> ITaikoL1.proveBlock() -> TaikoL1.proveBlock() hits whenProvingNotPaused modifier at line 29-32 which checks state.slotB.provingPaused. When provingPaused is true (set by pauseProving at line 100-103), the modifier reverts with L1_PROVING_PAUSED(). There is no conditional check for guardian-tier proofs, no alternative proveBlock function for guardians, and no bypass mechanism. The _checkProverPermission function in LibProving.sol lines 296-310 does have special handling for top-tier (contestBond == 0) proofs at line 299 ('if (_tier.contestBond == 0) return;'), but this is never reached because the revert happens at the TaikoL1.proveBlock() modifier level before LibProving.proveBlock() is even called.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects the proving pause behavior - the whenProvingNotPaused modifier is hardcoded in TaikoL1.proveBlock().

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is valid: 1) A malicious prover submits an invalid proof. 2) Owner or chain_pauser calls pauseProving(true) to prevent further damage. 3) Guardians accumulate approvals via GuardianProver.approve(). 4) The last guardian's approval triggers the threshold - approve() tries to call TaikoL1.proveBlock() which reverts with L1_PROVING_PAUSED. 5) Protocol is stuck: blocks cannot be verified, guardian corrective proofs cannot be submitted. 6) Owner must unpause proving, but this also re-enables all provers including potentially malicious ones, creating a race condition window. While the owner could theoretically unpause atomically with guardians submitting, there is no on-chain mechanism guaranteeing this atomicity.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact: When proving is paused during an emergency, the protocol enters a state where block verification is completely halted. The guardian tier—the only mechanism capable of resolving proof disputes with ultimate authority—is disabled. This creates a deadlock: the emergency measure (pause) prevents the emergency resolution mechanism (guardian proof) from functioning. All provers' bonds remain locked, L2 chain progression halts from the L1 perspective, and cross-chain state syncing via signal service stops. Recovery requires unpausing which opens a race condition window for malicious actors.
recommended_mitigations: Add a guardian-specific proving function to TaikoL1 that bypasses the whenProvingNotPaused check, only callable by the resolved guardian_prover address
Modify the whenProvingNotPaused modifier to exempt calls from the guardian prover: if (state.slotB.provingPaused && msg.sender != resolve('guardian_prover', true)) revert L1_PROVING_PAUSED();
Create a separate proveBlockByGuardian() function without the whenProvingNotPaused modifier but restricted to the guardian prover address
At minimum, document this limitation clearly so operators understand that unpausing is required before guardian intervention
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy TaikoL1 and GuardianProver with proper initialization. 2. Propose a block with proposeBlock(). 3. Call pauseProving(true) as owner. 4. Have guardians call GuardianProver.approve() with TIER_GUARDIAN proof. 5. When the last guardian triggers the threshold, observe the revert with L1_PROVING_PAUSED. 6. Verify that no guardian proof can be submitted while proving is paused.
uncertainties: The owner could potentially unpause proving and guardians could submit in the same block via flashbots/MEV bundle, providing atomicity off-chain, but this is not guaranteed and adds operational complexity
The severity depends on how quickly the owner can coordinate an unpause + guardian proof submission sequence
additional_testing_needed: Test GuardianProver.approve() behavior when proving is paused to confirm revert
Test the race condition window when unpausing: can a malicious prover front-run guardian proofs?
Test whether individual guardian approval bits are preserved across failed threshold attempts (they should be since the whole transaction reverts)
